name: Vijayindra Tirtha

dates_evidence.description: Royal-court records of the late Vijayanagara period.

core_thesis:
Vijayīndra Tīrtha is the late-sixteenth-century consolidator of Vyāsatīrtha's *Navya-Dvaita*. Tradition counts more than a hundred polemical works under his name (the round figure of 104 is canonical); how many of those are securely his is a separate philological question. The works principally engaged here are the three for which authorship is uncontested and which together index his distinctive contribution: the *Madhva-Tantra-Mukha-Mardana* (the reply to Appayya Dīkṣita's *Madhva-Tantra-Mukha-Bhañjana*), the *Nyāyāmṛta-Vyākhyā* on Vyāsatīrtha, and the *Nyāyādhva-Dīpikā*.

The doctrinal commitments are uniformly Mādhva: *svatantra-paratantra* hierarchy, *pañca-bheda*, *viśeṣa*, *bimba-pratibimba*, the rejection of *mithyātva* under all five canonical *lakṣaṇa*s. The contribution is twofold.

First, Vijayīndra extends the school's polemic to opponents Vyāsatīrtha had engaged less directly. The *Madhva-Tantra-Mukha-Mardana* answers Appayya Dīkṣita's Śivādvaita-inflected attack on Madhva's authenticity as a Vedāntin and on his use of disputed scriptural citations. The argument turns on the standing of *āpta-vākya* (authoritative utterance): Vijayīndra defends the *Pañcarātra* and *khila* materials Madhva relies on by arguing that *āpta-vākya* status is fixed by the speaker's epistemic standing, not by conventional textual canon.

Second, methodologically, he extends Vyāsatīrtha's *Navya-Nyāya* technique into finer-grained *vyāpti*-analysis, especially for disputed inferences over *bheda*, the standing of *sākṣi* as *pramāṇa*, and the *jīva*-classification *taratamya*. The *Nyāyāmṛta-Vyākhyā* anticipates and answers the objections that Madhusūdana's developing *Advaita-Siddhi* will press against the *Nyāyāmṛta*; many of Rāghavendra's *Pariṃala*-positions presuppose this prior dialectical work.

engaged_works.title: Nyayadhva-Dipika
engaged_works.summary:
A methodological *prakaraṇa* expounding the Mādhva doctrine of *pramāṇa* and *prameya* in *Navya-Dvaita* form. The text is set as a *dīpikā* on the *nyāya* — the *yukti-paddhati*, the school's method of reasoned construction — rather than on a particular treatise.

Three topics are treated. First, *anubhava-sākṣi* is fixed as a self-luminous *pramāṇa*. The argument is that the witness-faculty's verdicts are given prior to any inference and so cannot be displaced by inference; *bādha* (sublation) operates among mediated cognitions but does not reach into the immediate. Second, the conditions under which *anumāna* is and is not subject to *bādha* are specified; the analysis turns on whether the *hetu* operates within the same *adhikaraṇa* as the alleged defeater. Third, *vyāpti*-conditions are worked out for inferences whose *sādhya* is itself a relational property — *bheda* (difference), *taratamya* (graded ranking), *bimbatva* (the original-pole of the original-and-image relation). Standard *vyāpti*-form had been developed for monadic predicates; relational *sādhya*s require additional structure, and Vijayīndra supplies it.

The text also defends, against Appayya-line and Vivaraṇa-line critics, the legitimacy of the Mādhva use of disputed *Pañcarātra* and *khila*-citations in *śabda-pramāṇa*. The argument is that *āpta-vākya* status is a function of the speaker's epistemic standing, not of the conventional textual canon by which a corpus is recognised. Whether a given citation is found in the standardly transmitted *Ṛg-veda* is irrelevant to whether it is the utterance of an *āpta*.

Where Vijayīndra's polemical works deploy the Mādhva method against opponents, the *Nyāyādhva-Dīpikā* articulates the method itself.

engaged_works.title: Madhva-Tantra-Mukha-Mardana
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in direct response to Appayya Dīkṣita's Madhva-Tantra-Mukha-Bhañjana, locating the work in the late Vijayīndra period.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored polemic; the canonical Mādhva reply to Appayya Dīkṣita.

ascription_evidence.description: Stable manuscript transmission within the Mādhva maṭha system.

engaged_works.summary:
Vijayīndra's reply to Appayya Dīkṣita's *Madhva-Tantra-Mukha-Bhañjana* ("Smashing the Face of Madhva-Doctrine"). Appayya, writing from a Śivādvaita-inflected Advaita position, had mounted a sustained attack on Madhva's authenticity as a Vedāntin and on the citation-fabric of his works. The *Mardana* answers objection by objection.

Three fronts are engaged. First, the standing of the disputed *Pañcarātra*, *khila*, and *Bhāratīya*-affiliated citations on which Madhva's positive theology of Viṣṇu-as-*svatantra* rests. Vijayīndra argues that *āpta-vākya* status is fixed by the speaker's epistemic standing rather than by the conventional textual corpus in which a citation is found; the question whether a citation is in the *Ṛg-veda-saṃhitā* as standardly received is distinct from the question whether it is the utterance of an *āpta*. The argument's premise is that Mīmāṃsaka-style canon-restriction confuses two separate questions.

Second, Appayya's specific *adhikaraṇa*-readings of the *Brahma-Sūtra* in which Śiva is identified as *param-Brahman*. Each is rebutted philologically: the relevant *upakrama–upasaṃhāra* signals do not, on Vijayīndra's reading, support the Śivādvaita construal.

Third, the Śivādvaita strategy of absorbing apparent Vaiṣṇava *śruti* under a higher Śaiva-monist key. Vijayīndra argues that the strategy presupposes a *mithyātva*-doctrine for the apparent-Vaiṣṇava layer, and that the *mithyātva*-doctrine has been refuted independently in the Mādhva *Khaṇḍana*-triad and in the *Nyāyāmṛta*.

The work is the principal late-medieval Mādhva engagement with Śivādvaita.

engaged_works.title: Nyayamrta-Vyakhya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed across Vijayīndra's mature period in dialogue with the developing Madhusūdana-line response to the Nyāyāmṛta.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on Vyāsatīrtha's Nyāyāmṛta.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta* — the *Navya-Dvaita* magnum opus that gave the school's anti-Advaita polemic its definitive *Navya-Nyāya* form. Vijayīndra's *Vyākhyā* both clarifies and extends the *Nyāyāmṛta*'s arguments, working *parichcheda* by *parichcheda* through Vyāsatīrtha's refutation of the Advaitic doctrines of *mithyātva*, *bheda-niṣedha*, *avidyā*, *anirvacanīya-khyāti*, and *jīvanmukti*. Where Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi* had begun to appear as the formal Advaitic counter, Vijayīndra anticipates and rebuts the new objections in the *Vyākhyā*, maintaining the *Nyāyāmṛta*'s position with refined *vyāpti*-analysis and tighter *Navya-Nyāya* formulations. The text is the principal bridge between Vyāsatīrtha and Rāghavendra's later *Tarka-Tāṇḍava-Pariṃala* on the Naiyāyika side and *Nyāya-Sudhā-Pariṃala* on the *Anuvyākhyāna* side: many of Rāghavendra's settled positions presuppose Vijayīndra's prior dialectical work in this *Vyākhyā*. Together with the *Madhva-Tantra-Mukha-Mardana* it constitutes the spine of Vijayīndra's polemical output on the school's two most active fronts (Advaita and Śivādvaita) in the late sixteenth century.
